curriculum.subject: Chemistry
curriculum.overallDescription: 11 units, 9 chapters, 71 topics, 10 subtopics, and 31 learning outcomes extracted through raw extraction, document-structure hierarchy building, node enrichment, normalized teaching blocks, validation, and statistics generation.
coreObjectives: Define and apply thermodynamic terms including system, surroundings, state functions, and process variables.
Calculate internal energy change (∆U) and enthalpy change (∆H) using calorimetric data.
Analyze reaction enthalpies and classify reactions based on heat absorption or release.
Determine the spontaneity of chemical processes using Gibbs Energy Change (∆G).
Correlate equilibrium constants with Gibbs energy and predict system behavior under varying conditions.
Distinguish between physical and chemical equilibria and explain the concept of dynamic equilibrium.
Apply the Law of Chemical Equilibrium to calculate equilibrium constants (K) for homogeneous and heterogeneous reactions.
Predict shifts in equilibrium position using Le Chatelier's principle in response to concentration, temperature, and pressure changes.
Analyze ionic equilibria involving acids, bases, salts, buffer solutions, and solubility products.
Evaluate the relationship between reaction quotient (Q), equilibrium constant (K), and Gibbs energy (G).
Identify oxidizing and reducing agents using classical definitions of oxidation and reduction.
Determine oxidation numbers and apply electron transfer concepts to balance redox reactions.

practicals.details: Observation of Le Chatelier's principle by varying ion concentrations.

competency_groups.title: Thermodynamics and Energy Transformations
competency_groups.description: Ability to apply thermodynamic principles to analyze energy changes, predict reaction spontaneity, and understand equilibrium states in chemical systems.
competencies: Define and apply thermodynamic terms including system, surroundings, state functions, and process variables.
Calculate internal energy change (∆U) and enthalpy change (∆H) using calorimetric data.
Analyze reaction enthalpies and classify reactions based on heat absorption or release.
Determine the spontaneity of chemical processes using Gibbs Energy Change (∆G).
Correlate equilibrium constants with Gibbs energy and predict system behavior under varying conditions.

competency_groups.title: Chemical Equilibrium and Ionic Systems
competency_groups.description: Competency in understanding dynamic equilibrium, applying the law of chemical equilibrium, and analyzing factors affecting ionic systems.
competencies: Distinguish between physical and chemical equilibria and explain the concept of dynamic equilibrium.
Apply the Law of Chemical Equilibrium to calculate equilibrium constants (K) for homogeneous and heterogeneous reactions.
Predict shifts in equilibrium position using Le Chatelier's principle in response to concentration, temperature, and pressure changes.
Analyze ionic equilibria involving acids, bases, salts, buffer solutions, and solubility products.
Evaluate the relationship between reaction quotient (Q), equilibrium constant (K), and Gibbs energy (G).

competency_groups.title: Redox Reactions and Electrode Processes
competency_groups.description: Ability to identify oxidation-reduction processes, balance redox equations, and understand electrode mechanisms.
competencies: Identify oxidizing and reducing agents using classical definitions of oxidation and reduction.
Determine oxidation numbers and apply electron transfer concepts to balance redox reactions.
Analyze redox reactions in the context of electrochemical cells and electrode processes.
Correlate redox potential with reaction spontaneity and feasibility.

competency_groups.title: Organic Chemistry Principles and Techniques
competency_groups.description: Competency in understanding the fundamental principles of organic chemistry, nomenclature, isomerism, and analytical techniques.
competencies: Represent organic structures using various notations and understand tetravalence of carbon.
Classify organic compounds and apply IUPAC nomenclature rules systematically.
Identify different types of isomerism and analyze structural relationships.
Explain fundamental concepts of organic reaction mechanisms including electron movement.
Apply methods for purification, qualitative analysis, and quantitative estimation of organic compounds.
competency_groups.unit_name: Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles and Techniques
competency_groups.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles and Techniques

competency_groups.title: Hydrocarbons and Their Properties
competency_groups.description: Ability to classify hydrocarbons, understand their structures, reactions, and assess environmental impacts.
competencies: Classify hydrocarbons into alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, and aromatic compounds based on structure.
Predict physical properties and chemical reactivity of different hydrocarbon classes.
Analyze the carcinogenic nature and toxicity associated with specific organic compounds.
Relate molecular structure to functional behavior in hydrocarbon systems.

competency_groups.title: s & p Block Elements Trends
competency_groups.description: Competency in analyzing periodic trends, electronic configurations, and unique properties of s and p block elements.
competencies: Correlate electronic configuration with position in the periodic table for s and p block elements.
Analyze trends in atomic/ionic radii, ionization enthalpy, and hydration enthalpy across periods and down groups.
Explain the unique behavior of the first element in each group compared to subsequent members.
Predict chemical properties based on periodic trends and electronic structure.

competency_groups.title: Gaseous State and Gas Laws
competency_groups.description: Ability to apply gas laws qualitatively, understand the ideal gas equation, and analyze deviations from ideal behavior.
competencies: Apply qualitative gas laws (Boyle's, Charles's, Avogadro's) to predict changes in gas properties.
Derive and utilize the ideal gas equation for gaseous systems.
Analyze real gas behavior and identify factors causing deviations from the ideal gas law.
Relate molecular motion to macroscopic gas properties.

competency_groups.title: Practical Skills and Laboratory Techniques
competency_groups.description: Competency in executing laboratory procedures, performing volumetric and salt analysis, and conducting scientific investigations.
competencies: Demonstrate basic laboratory techniques including cutting, bending glass, and boring corks.
Determine physical constants such as melting point and boiling point of organic compounds.
Perform crystallization to purify impure chemical samples.
Conduct pH-based experiments and study equilibrium shifts in chemical systems.
Execute quantitative estimation using titration methods (e.g., Oxalic acid vs. NaOH).
Identify cations and anions in salts through qualitative analysis procedures.
Detect elements like Nitrogen, Sulphur, and Chlorine in organic compounds.

competency_groups.title: Scientific Inquiry and Project Work
competency_groups.description: Ability to design and execute scientific investigations, analyze data, and apply chemical knowledge to real-world problems.
competencies: Design and conduct investigatory projects involving laboratory testing and data collection.
Analyze water quality parameters including bacterial contamination, hardness, and ion presence.
Evaluate the foaming capacity of soaps and the effect of additives.
Investigate acidity in natural samples like tea leaves and fruit juices.
Study physical phenomena such as evaporation rates and tensile strength effects.

Analyze redox reactions in the context of electrochemical cells and electrode processes.
Correlate redox potential with reaction spontaneity and feasibility.

learning_outcomes.unit_name: Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles and Techniques
learning_outcomes.chapter_name: Organic Chemistry – Some Basic Principles and Techniques
outcomes: Represent organic structures using various notations and understand tetravalence of carbon.
Classify organic compounds and apply IUPAC nomenclature rules systematically.
Identify different types of isomerism and analyze structural relationships.
Explain fundamental concepts of organic reaction mechanisms including electron movement.
Apply methods for purification, qualitative analysis, and quantitative estimation of organic compounds.
Classify hydrocarbons into alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, and aromatic compounds based on structure.
Predict physical properties and chemical reactivity of different hydrocarbon classes.
Analyze the carcinogenic nature and toxicity associated with specific organic compounds.
Relate molecular structure to functional behavior in hydrocarbon systems.
Correlate electronic configuration with position in the periodic table for s and p block elements.
Analyze trends in atomic/ionic radii, ionization enthalpy, and hydration enthalpy across periods and down groups.
Explain the unique behavior of the first element in each group compared to subsequent members.
Predict chemical properties based on periodic trends and electronic structure.
Apply qualitative gas laws (Boyle's, Charles's, Avogadro's) to predict changes in gas properties.
Derive and utilize the ideal gas equation for gaseous systems.
Analyze real gas behavior and identify factors causing deviations from the ideal gas law.
Relate molecular motion to macroscopic gas properties.

marks_distribution: Unit 1: Some Basic Concepts of Chemistry - 7 Marks
Unit 3: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties - 6 Marks
Unit 4: Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure - 7 Marks
Unit 8: Organic Chemistry: Some basic Principles and Techniques - 11 Marks
question_paper_design: Formative Assessment Topics (No Summative Weightage): s & p Block Elements, The Gaseous State
competency_weightage: Atomic Models and Quantum Mechanical Model
Chemical Bonding Theories (Kossel-Lewis, VSEPR, Valence Bond, Molecular Orbital)
Thermodynamic Terms, Enthalpy Change, Spontaneity, Gibbs Energy
Equilibrium Constants, Reaction Quotient, Factors Affecting Equilibria
bloom_levels: Knowledge: Laws of Chemical Combination, Atomic Theory, Periodic Law
Understanding: Bohr's Model, VSEPR Theory, Bonding Types
Application: Stoichiometric Calculations, pH Determination, Titration
Analysis: Electronic Configuration Trends, Equilibrium Shifts
Evaluation: Purification Methods, Project Investigations
internal_assessment: Formative Assessment for s & p Block Elements (No marks in final theory exam)
Formative Assessment for The Gaseous State (No marks in final theory exam)

syllabus_areas: Basic Laboratory Techniques (Cutting, Bending, Drawing, Boring)
Characterization and Purification (Melting/Boiling Point, Crystallization)
Experiments based on pH (Fruit juices, Strong/Weak acids, Titration)
Chemical Equilibrium (Ferric-Thiocyanate, Cobalt-Chloride)
Quantitative Estimation (Balance usage, Standard solutions, Titrations)
Qualitative Analysis (Cation/Anion detection, Organic elements)
